title: Peace on Pause: Israel and Lebanon Extend Ceasefire, but Hezbollah Says No
summary:
Have you ever tried to get two friends to stop arguing, only to have a third person keep stirring things up? That's basically the situation between Israel and Lebanon right now — except the stakes are life and death.

On Thursday, President Trump announced that Israel and Lebanon had agreed to extend their ceasefire (a temporary halt to fighting) by three weeks. The initial 10-day ceasefire, which started last Friday, was about to expire on Monday. The deal came after a meeting at the White House between the Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors, with Trump, Vice President JD Vance, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio looking on.

"The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah," Trump said in a social media post. He also said he hopes to meet in person with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Lebanese President Joseph Aoun "in the near future."

Lebanese Ambassador Nada Hamadeh Moawad thanked Trump directly: "I want to really say thank you to the United States, under your leadership, for all your effort to help and to support Lebanon. And I think with your help, with your support, we can make Lebanon great."

But here's the catch: Hezbollah, an Iranian-backed militant group that is very powerful in Lebanon, has rejected the talks entirely. Wafiq Safa, a high-ranking member of Hezbollah's political council, told the Associated Press that the group will not abide by any agreements made during the direct talks. This is a huge obstacle, because Hezbollah controls much of southern Lebanon and has been the main force fighting Israel.

Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar called on Lebanon to work with Israel to disarm Hezbollah. "The obstacle to peace and normalization between the countries is one: Hezbollah," Saar said.

The latest war started when Hezbollah fired rockets into northern Israel, two days after Israel and the U.S. launched attacks on Iran. Israel responded with massive bombardment and a ground invasion, capturing dozens of towns and villages along the border. Israel's military currently occupies a buffer zone stretching up to 10 kilometers (6 miles) into southern Lebanon.

Since the ceasefire began, there have been multiple violations by both sides. On Wednesday, a well-known Lebanese journalist named Amal Khalil was killed by an Israeli strike while covering southern Lebanon. Lebanese health officials said the Israeli military opened fire on an ambulance that responded, preventing rescuers from reaching her. The Israeli military denied deliberately targeting journalists, but the case sparked widespread anger in Lebanon.

The war has killed around 2,300 people in Lebanon, including hundreds of women and children, and displaced over 1 million people. The Lebanese government hopes these talks will pave the way to a permanent end to the fighting. But with Hezbollah refusing to cooperate, that path looks anything but smooth.
why_it_matters: This ceasefire extension reflects the fragile state of Middle Eastern geopolitics, where non-state actors like Hezbollah can derail peace processes and civilian lives hang in the balance.

keywords.explanation: a temporary halt to fighting, often used to allow negotiations
an Iranian-backed militant group that controls parts of southern Lebanon
a news reporter, such as Amal Khalil who was killed while covering the conflict
the official residence and workplace of the U.S. president
negotiations between parties aimed at resolving disagreements
a large-scale armed conflict between countries or groups

questions.question: How long was the initial ceasefire before the extension?
Which group rejected the direct talks between Israel and Lebanon?
What did the Israeli military deny about the incident involving Amal Khalil?
How many people have been displaced in Lebanon due to the war?
What did Trump say the U.S. would help Lebanon with?
According to the text, what is a major obstacle to peace between Israel and Lebanon?

background_read: Israel and Lebanon have a long history of conflict. In 2006, a 34-day war between Israel and Hezbollah killed over 1,000 people in Lebanon and 160 in Israel. Since then, UN Resolution 1701 called for Hezbollah disarmament and removal of foreign forces, but it was never fully implemented.

Hezbollah was formed in 1982 during Lebanon’s civil war with support from Iran. It is considered a terrorist group by the U.S. and many other countries, but also has political power in Lebanon, holding seats in parliament and providing social services.
The recent escalation is linked to the Israel-Hamas war in Gaza. Hezbollah opened a front in solidarity with Hamas in October 2023, leading to cross-border attacks and Israeli retaliations.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Analogy about friends arguing with a third party stirring things up
└─ specific: 'the stakes are life and death'
KEY EVENT: Trump announces 3-week ceasefire extension at White House meeting
├─ WHO: Israeli and Lebanese ambassadors, Trump, Vance, Rubio
├─ WHEN: Thursday; initial 10-day ceasefire started last Friday
├─ HEZBOLLAH REJECTION: Wafiq Safa says group won't abide by agreements
└─ ISRAELI DEMAND: Foreign Minister Saar calls for Lebanon to disarm Hezbollah
EVIDENCE OF VIOLATIONS: Journalist Amal Khalil killed; Israeli army denied targeting
OPEN QUESTION: Will Hezbollah's defiance prevent lasting peace?

perspectives.description: The extension provides a window for serious negotiations. Direct talks between Israel and Lebanon, with U.S. mediation, could lead to a permanent end to hostilities and allow displaced families to return home.

Hezbollah’s refusal to participate undermines the agreement. Without their cooperation, any ceasefire is just a pause. The killing of a journalist also shows that violations continue, and trust remains low.

This pattern is familiar: ceasefires in the Israel-Lebanon context have historically failed due to Hezbollah’s independence and Israel’s security concerns. The current arrangement is a temporary measure, not a solution.

For lasting peace, Lebanon must integrate Hezbollah into national security structures or disarm it. The U.S. may push for economic incentives to weaken Hezbollah's influence, but that would require significant political will from all sides.
